coredata.dc:title: GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid: A novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent probe for H2O2 and oxidase-based visual biosensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jie-Qiong
Xue, Shi-Fan
Chen, Zi-Han
Zhang, Shengqiang
Shi, Guoyue
Zhang, Min
coredata.dc:description: A novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent probe is developed for the detection of H2O2 based on GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid, in which GelRed (a nucleic acid dye) intercalated into the designed single-stranded DNA [G3T]5 (i.e. GelRed/[G3T]5) acts as a stable build-in reference with red emission and Tb3+ sensitized by [G3T]5 (i.e. [G3T]5/Tb3+) as a sensitive response signal with green fluorescence. With the successive addition of Hg2+ and cysteine (Cys) to GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+, the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+ can be effectively quenched and recovered respectively, while the fluorescence of GelRed/[G3T]5 remains unchangeable. Combined with these properties, we have demonstrated its application for label-free ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 with self-calibration. The sensing mechanism is based on the specific reaction between H2O2 and Cys, the resulting disulfide reverses the Cys-mediated fluorescence changes of [G3T]5/Tb3+. This method is further applied to the monitoring of oxidase-related reactions. As the glucose oxidase (GOx)-biocatalyzed oxidation of glucose and the acetylcholine esterase/choline oxidase (AChE/ChOx) cascade yield H2O2, such two biocatalytic processes are successfully examined utilized our proposed method, indicating its potential broad applications in biomedical analysis. In addition, we have also demonstrated its feasibility for oxidase-based visual biosensing, in which only a handheld UV lamp is used. Importantly, coupled with our proposed visual biosensing, a realtime quantitative scanometric assays has been also demonstrated by using a smartphone with easy-to-access color-scanning APP as the detection platform. Compared to traditional methods, the proposed design is cost-effective, simple to prepare and easy-to-use without fluorescence labeling or chemical modification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GelRed
DNA
Tb3+
Label-free ratiometric
H2O2
Oxidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the sensing system for detection of H2O2
Assays for glucose and AChE/ChOx cascade reactions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21405047 , 21775044 , 21675053 and 21635003 ). M. Zhang also thanks the support from the China Scholarship Council, Ministry of Education of China ( 201706145029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescent emission spectra of a) GelRed/[G3T]5; b) GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+; c) GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+-Hg2+; d) GelRed /[G3T]5/Tb3+-Hg2+-Cys; e) GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+-Hg2+-Cys-H2O2. The concentrations of GelRed, [G3T]5, Tb3+, Hg2+, Cys and H2O2 were 0.3X, 4µM, 10µM, 25µM, 30µM and 100µM, respectively. (B) The corresponding fluorescence photograph of the solution under the ultraviolet radiation using a smart phone.

The determination of H2O2 via reversing the Cys-mediated fluorescence changes in the sensing system. (A) Fluorescent responses of the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ -Hg2+-Cys system to H2O2 at increasing concentrations (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 and 150μM). (B) Plot of fluorescent responses to the various concentrations of H2O2 indicated. (C) The corresponding fluorescence photograph of the sensing solution under the ultraviolet radiation. (D) The application of a color-scanning APP in H2O2 detection, and plot of R/G values to the various concentrations of H2O2.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ -Hg2+-Cys–GOx system in the presence of different concentrations of glucose (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100 and 150µM). (B) Plot of F546/F650 to the various concentrations of glucose indicated. (C) The corresponding fluorescence photograph of the probe solution under the ultraviolet radiation. (D) The application of a color-scanning APP in glucose detection, and plot of R/G values to the various concentrations of glucose.

Selectivity and competition analysis for glucose. (A) Fluorescent spectra of the GelRed /[G3T]5 /Tb3+ -Hg2+ -Cys -GOx sensing system with glucose (100µM) and other sugars (500µM). (B) Bars represent the fluorescence intensity ratio F546/F650 to all sugars. (C) Fluorescence responses of 100µM glucose in the presence of 500µM other sugars. (D) Bars represent the fluorescence intensity ratio F546/F650 to 100µM glucose in the presence of 500µM other sugars.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of label-free ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for H2O2 and oxidase-based biosensing.

head.ce:title: GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid: A novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent probe for H2O2 and oxidase-based visual biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University, 500 Dongchuan Road, Shanghai 200241, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Shanghai Key Laboratory for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Chemical Engineering, Sanming University, Sanming 365004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Chemical Engineering, Sanming University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent probe is developed for the detection of H2O2 based on GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid, in which GelRed (a nucleic acid dye) intercalated into the designed single-stranded DNA [G3T]5 (i.e. GelRed/[G3T]5) acts as a stable build-in reference with red emission and Tb3+ sensitized by [G3T]5 (i.e. [G3T]5/Tb3+) as a sensitive response signal with green fluorescence. With the successive addition of Hg2+ and cysteine (Cys) to GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+, the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+ can be effectively quenched and recovered respectively, while the fluorescence of GelRed/[G3T]5 remains unchangeable. Combined with these properties, we have demonstrated its application for label-free ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 with self-calibration. The sensing mechanism is based on the specific reaction between H2O2 and Cys, the resulting disulfide reverses the Cys-mediated fluorescence changes of [G3T]5/Tb3+. This method is further applied to the monitoring of oxidase-related reactions. As the glucose oxidase (GOx)-biocatalyzed oxidation of glucose and the acetylcholine esterase/choline oxidase (AChE/ChOx) cascade yield H2O2, such two biocatalytic processes are successfully examined utilized our proposed method, indicating its potential broad applications in biomedical analysis. In addition, we have also demonstrated its feasibility for oxidase-based visual biosensing, in which only a handheld UV lamp is used. Importantly, coupled with our proposed visual biosensing, a realtime quantitative scanometric assays has been also demonstrated by using a smartphone with easy-to-access color-scanning APP as the detection platform. Compared to traditional methods, the proposed design is cost-effective, simple to prepare and easy-to-use without fluorescence labeling or chemical modification.

ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 plays critical roles in maintaining the physiological balance of organisms in the living body (Chen et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). Misregulation of H2O2 can result in severe damage for proteins and DNA, thus further associating with various pathological events such as chronic inflammation (Salvemimi et al., 2006), Alzheimer (Huang and Mucke, 2012), diabetes (Houstis et al., 2006), neuropathology (Mazzio and Soliman, 2004) and cancer (Finkel et al., 2007). Therefore, it is of practical importance for the detection of H2O2 in the biological and clinical environments. Additionally, H2O2 is a metabolic by-product from reactions catalyzed by oxidases such as glucose oxidase (GOx), choline oxidase (ChOx), and so on, which makes H2O2 an important intermediate to follow in biochemical processes (Wang et al., 2013; Chang and Ho, 2015). Based on these facts, the methods for H2O2 detection can open the new window for the sensing of the oxidation reaction-related biomolecules.

Varied methods have been recently reported for the detection of H2O2, such as colorimetry (Yang and Tseng, 2016), chemiluminescence (Lee et al., 2012), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Gu et al., 2016), electrochemistry (Ju and Chen, 2015) and fluorescence (Song et al., 2016). Among these, fluorescence-based technologies have sparked tremendous interest due to their simplicity, sensitivity and good reproducibility. To date, various fluorescent probes, such as organic dye (Liu et al., 2015a, 2015b), quantum dots (Zhao et al., 2016) and nanoclusters (Liu et al., 2017) have been developed for H2O2 detection. Although these fluorescent probes have achieved impressive results, some drawbacks still exist, such as complicated syntheses, high costs and unsatisfactory detection limits. Thus, it is highly desirable to develop novel strategies for fluorescent assay to H2O2, e.g. in a label-free format or a ratiometric mode. Label-free fluorescent probes, by definition, do not require the labeling of reporter elements to facilitate measurements, and has become a powerful tool in the hands of biochemical and environmental analysts Zhang et al., 2013a, 2013b; Jiang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2016). Ratiometric fluorescence measurement, which could provide two significant emission signals at different wavelengths under the same excitation wavelength, can provide an intrinsic built-in correction to the environment effects and thus possess advantages in terms of improved sensitivity and accuracy (Teng et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015, 2017; Yu et al., 2017). Moreover, it would be promising to combine the advantages of both label-free and ratiometric measurements, but there are few reports about fluorescent assays in a label-free ratiometric manner.

Herein, we present a novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent assay for H2O2 by using GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid ( Scheme 1). The fluorescence of Tb3+ is weak owing to low absorption cross sections and nonradiative deactivation through the O-H vibrations of coordinated water molecules. According to previous studies, the single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) can greatly enhance the Tb3+ emission resulting from energy transfer from ssDNA to Tb3+ (Fu and Turro, 1999), and Tb3+ could bind with two sites of ssDNA: the phosphate moieties and electron donor groups on the nucleoside bases. In our previous work, an optimum ssDNA antenna ligand ([G3T]5) was identified for effective sensitization of the fluorescence of Tb3+ (Zhang et al., 2013a, 2013b, 2014a). In our proposed hybrid, GelRed intercalated into [G3T]5 (i.e. GelRed/[G3T]5) acts as a stable build-in reference with red emission and Tb3+ sensitized by [G3T]5 (i.e. [G3T]5/Tb3+) as a sensitive response signal with green fluorescence. It is well known that Hg2+ can specifically interact with thymine (T) bases to form strong and stable T-Hg2+-T complexes (Zhang et al., 2011). Since [G3T]5 interacts with Hg2+ that weakens its sensitizing ability to Tb3+, the green fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+ can be quenched; meanwhile, the red fluorescence of GelRed/[G3T]5 shows negligible changes. With the further addition of Cys, Hg2+ is captured through the interaction between the thiol group of Cys and Hg2+, resulting in the fluorescent recovery of [G3T]5/Tb3+. The oxidation of Cys to cystine by H2O2 (Wang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014a, 2014b) reverses the Cys-mediated fluorescence changes of [G3T]5/Tb3+, which promises a label-free ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 with self-calibration. Moreover, as H2O2 is a by-product generated in the oxidation reaction catalyzed by many oxidases (Lu et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2017), the sensing platform is demonstrated for detecting oxidation reaction-related biomolecules, e.g. glucose. As the GOx-biocatalyzed oxidation of glucose and the AChE/ChOx cascade yield H2O2, the biocatalytic processes are successfully probed utilized our proposed method. Additionally, by virtue of a color-scanning APP, RGB intensities of fluorescence color image can be directly obtained using a smartphone, which promotes to the real-time quantitative scanometric assay.

The oligonucleotide ([G3T]5: 5′-GGGTGGGTGGGTGGGTGGGT-3′) used in this study was synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Terbium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Tb(NO3)3) was purchased from Diyang Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GelRed (10000X) in water was purchased from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). L-asparagine (L-Asn), L-aspartic acid (L-Asp), L-leucine (L-Leu), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-alanine (L-Ala), L-isoleucine (L-Ile), L-tyrosine (L-Tyr), L-valine (L-Val), L-threonine (L-Thr), Glycine (Gly), DL-phenylalanine (DL-Phe), L-glutamine (L-Gln), L-serine (L-Ser), L-proline (L-Pro), L-histidine (L-His), L-tryptophan (L-Trp), L-lysine (L-Lys), L-methionine (L-Met), L-arginine (L-Arg), L-cysteine (L-Cys), acetylcholine esterase (AChE) and acetyl choline (ACh) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Glucose oxidase (GOx) and choline oxidase (ChOx) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Glucose was purchased from Qiangsheng Chemical (Jiangsu, China) Co., Ltd. Hg(Ac)2, fructose (Fru), lactose (Lac), sucrose (Suc), maltose (Mat), and mannose (Man) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose assay kit (Glu Kit) was purchased from Shanghai Mind Bioengineering Co, Ltd. All the solutions were prepared in ultrapure water purified by a Milli-Q purification system (18.2MΩcm−1, Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA). Tris-HAc buffer (100mM, pH = 7.9) was prepared by adding certain amounts of Tris in Milli-Q system and then the pH value of the buffer was adjusted by titrating with HAc. All reagents are analytical purity grade and used directly without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded in a microplate reader system (infinite M200 pro, TECAN, Switzerland) using a black 384-well microplate (Corning, U.S.A.). The excitation wavelength used was 290nm for the emission spectra. A gate time of 2ms was used for the fluorescence spectra recording. Fluorescence color image was taken with a smartphone under the 254nm ultraviolet radiation, and then RGB intensities of the image can be directly output by using a custom developed color-scanning APP (World of Color, Maarten Zonneveld), which can be readily downloaded to the smartphone online.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the sensing system for detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ -Hg2+ sensing system (GelRed, [G3T]5, Tb3+ and Hg2+ were used) was prepared in 10mM Tris–HAc buffer (pH=7.9) to form a composite reagent. For the detection of H2O2, a series of different concentrations of H2O2 (0–150µM) was mixed with Cys and then incubated for 20min at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was added to the as-prepared composite system and then incubated for 10min at room temperature after blending completely. The final concentration of GelRed, [G3T]5, Tb3+, Hg2+ and Cys was 0.3X, 4µM, 10µM, 25μM and 30μM, respectively. After that, an aliquot of 90μL mixture was placed in the black 384 well microplate to measure the fluorescence intensity (excited at 290nm, a gate time of 2ms). Additionally, a series of noticeable color changes from green to red was observed with the naked eyes under the 254nm ultraviolet radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assays for glucose and AChE/ChOx cascade reactions
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay of glucose, an amount of GOx, Cys and different concentrations of glucose (0–150µM) was mixed with 10mM Tris–HAc buffer (pH=7.9) and incubated for 30min at room temperature. Then, the reaction mixture was added into the as-prepared composite reagent and then incubated for 10min at room temperature after blending completely. The final concentration of GelRed, [G3T]5, Tb3+, Hg2+, Cys and GOx was 0.3X, 4µM, 10µM, 25μM, 30μM and 0.1mg/mL, respectively. After that, an aliquot of 90μL mixture was placed in the black 384 well microplate to measure the fluorescence intensity (excited at 290nm, a gate time of 2ms). Serum samples from healthy male rats were used to verify the practicality of this probe. The blood collecting procedures were performed in compliance with relevant laws and institutional guidelines. Serum samples were obtained after centrifuging (8000rpm, 15min, 4°C) fresh blood samples after standing for 2h. High concentration of serum is unsuitable for our work because of the detection range, so the original serum samples were diluted with ultrapure water for following experiments. Different concentrations of glucose were spiked into a series of serum using spike-in method. Subsequently, those samples were added into the detection system. The following steps were same as those procedures mentioned above for glucose detection. For AChE/ChOx cascade assay, an amount of AChE, ChOx, Cys and different concentrations of ACh was mixed with 10mM Tris–HAc buffer (pH = 7.9) and incubated for 140min at room temperature. The final concentration of GelRed, [G3T]5, Tb3+, Hg2+, Cys, AChE and ChOx was 0.3X, 4µM, 10µM, 25μM, 30μM, 0.125U/mL and 0.25U/mL, respectively. The subsequent steps were the same as those of glucose detection.

We first construct the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid, which is then regulated by Hg2+ and Cys to demonstrate the feasibility of our design. GelRed is widely used as a sensitive dye in nucleic acid-intercalated staining in gel electrophoresis (Crisafuli et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2011; Haines et al., 2015). The free GelRed is weakly fluorescent. When the ssDNA ([G3T]5) is mixed with GelRed, the mixed solution exhibits the strong red fluorescence under ultraviolet radiation ( Fig. 1B, tube a). According to previous works, guanine can greatly enhance the Tb3+ emission because its triplet energy state overlaps with the resonance energy levels of Tb3+ (Formoso, 1973). In the presence of [G3T]5, the fluorescent intensity of Tb3+ is increased significantly and shows enhanced green fluorescence. When the solution of GelRed, [G3T]5 and Tb3+ are mixed together to form the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid, it will exhibit green fluorescence by regulating the concentration of Tb3+ and GelRed (Fig. 1B, tube b). With the addition of Hg2+, the [G3T]5-sensitized fluorescence of Tb3+ ([G3T]5/Tb3+) is effectively quenched while the fluorescence of GelRed/[G3T]5 is stable. As a result, the fluorescence of the hybrid exhibits red color (Fig. 1B, tube c). Upon further addition of Cys to the above system, Hg2+ is grabbed from T-Hg2+-T complex and then turn on the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+, thus the hybrid exhibits green color again (Fig. 1B, tube d). Moreover, the oxidation of Cys to cystine by H2O2 reverses the Cys-mediated fluorescence changes of [G3T]5/Tb3+ (Fig. 1B, tube e). As shown in Fig. 1A, in the mixture solution of GelRed and ssDNA, the emission peak of GelRed at 640nm is observed (line a), and in the presence of Tb3+, the characteristic emission peaks of Tb3+ at 491, 546, 585 and 620nm are observed (line b). With the addition of Hg2+ and Cys, the emission peak of Tb3+ is quenched (line c) and recovered (line d), respectively. After adding the H2O2, the emission peak of Tb3+ decreases again (line e). During the whole process, no obvious fluorescence signal change at 650nm is obtained, indicating GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+-Hg2+-Cys sensing system can be used as the label-free ratiometric fluorescent probe for H2O2 with good performance.

In order to improve the sensitivity of the sensing system toward H2O2, we have optimized some important parameters, including the concentration of Tb3+, Hg2+ and Cys. We first investigated the effect of Tb3+ concentration on the fluorescent behavior of the hybrid. With the increase addition of Tb3+, as indicated in Fig. S1, the fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) gradually reached its saturation level above 10µM Tb3+. On the basis of superior GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+, different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–35µM) were added in Tris-HAc buffer (10mM, pH 7.9). As shown in Fig. S2, the presence of increasing concentrations of Hg2+ to the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ caused the gradual fluorescent decrease of [G3T]5/Tb3+ and the color changed from green to red. Moreover, the fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) nearly completely achieved plateau value upon incubated with 25µM Hg2+, and the quenching efficiency reached an equilibrium at 92.06%. Thus, 25µM Hg2+ was considered as an optimal concentration to the sensing system for quenching the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+. To test selectivity, competing stimuli including various cations at 25µM concentrations were examined under the same conditions (Fig. S3). Upon further addition of various concentrations of Cys (0–40µM) to the above system, Hg2+ is grabbed from T-Hg2+-T complex and then turn on the Tb3+ fluorescence (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S4B, the fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) was nearly completely recovered upon incubated with 30µM Cys. Thus, 30µM Cys was considered as an optimal concentration to the sensing system for recovering the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+. Selectivity and competition analysis of Cys-mediated fluorescent recovery was evaluated by examining the F546/F650 of the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+-Hg2+ in the presence of various amino acids, GSH and Hcys under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S5, only the addition of biothiols could induce a prominent increase, whereas the addition of 3.3-fold 19 other amino acids only caused very slight influences. In a further experiment, we investigated the stability of the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid. As shown in Fig. S6, the fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) remains with no apparent change within 64h, implying that the hybrid probe has good stability.

Using the optimal experimental conditions obtained above, the responses of the sensing system to H2O2 ranging from 0 to 150µM were evaluated. While Cys was premixed with H2O2 and then added into the composite reagent, the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+ at 546nm was quenched, and the fluorescence of GelRed/[G3T]5 at 650nm kept unchanged ( Fig. 2A). Fig. 2B shows the calibration curve based on the fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) versus H2O2 concentration. In the linear region (0–60µM), the regression equation is Y = − 0.1766X + 12.74 with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.99, where Y and X represent the fluorescent intensity ratio F546/F650 and the concentration of H2O2, respectively. The detection limit of our proposed label-free ratiometric probe for H2O2 was estimated to be 2.11µM (3σ/slope, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples). As shown in Fig. 2C, upon exposure to different concentrations of H2O2, an obvious fluorescence color switch from green to red could be readily observed by naked eyes under a 254nm UV lamp. Fluorescence color image was taken with a smartphone, and then a color-scanning APP was used to record red (R) and green (G) color intensities of the image. Then the R/G ratio was calculated to evaluate the difference among varied concentrations of H2O2 (Fig. 2D). The calibration curve based on the R/G ratio versus H2O2 concentration (0–100µM) can be obtained, the regression equation is Y = 0.0058X + 0.75 with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.9922, where Y and X represent the R/G and the concentration of H2O2, respectively. The lowest detectable concentration (LDC) was approximately 3.6µM, which is similar with the detection limit of the above ratiometric fluorescent assays.

Many biological substrates, such as glucose and choline, play essential roles in physiological reactions and human health. While specific oxidases can oxidize these substrates to generate H2O2 in the presence of O2. Glucose is important in biochemical processes and acts as an energy source in living body to maintain human functioning (Huang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2015a, 2015b). The successful sensitive detection of H2O2 was further extended for the analysis of glucose by using the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ -Hg2+-Cys sensing system, in which GOx can catalyze the oxidation of glucose to yield H2O2 (1). The resulting H2O2 oxidizes Cys to cystine, which can give rise to the fluorescent change of the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ -Hg2+-Cys–GOx system.
(1)
Glucose + O
2
⟶
GOx
Gluconic
acid + H
2
O
2

To explore this attractive possibility, the reaction time of GOx-mediated enzymatic reaction was firstly optimized to obtain optimum performance, because it will affect the amount of produced H2O2. Fig. S7 shows that the fluorescent ratio can decrease with time and reach plateau after reaction for 30min at a fixed concentration of glucose. Choosing 30min as reaction time, the sensitivity of GOx-mediated glucose detection was evaluated using the above optimum conditions. As the glucose concentrations increased from 0 to 150µM at a fixed concentration of GOx, the fluorescent intensity at 546nm progressively reduced while the emission of GelRed/[G3T]5 was stable ( Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows the calibration curve based on the fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) versus glucose concentration the same as H2O2. In the linear region (10–50µM), the regression equation is Y = − 0.1974X + 14.51 with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.9921, where Y and X represents the fluorescent intensity ratio F546/F650 and the concentration of glucose, respectively. The detection limit of this label-free ratiometric probe for glucose was calculated to be 1.43µM (3σ/slope, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples). As shown in Fig. 3C, with the increase of glucose concentration (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100µM), the color switch from green to red could be readily observed by naked eyes under a 254nm UV lamp. Fluorescence color image was taken with a smartphone, and then a color-scanning APP was used to scan red (R) and green (G) color intensities of the image. Then the R/G ratio was calculated to evaluate the difference among varied concentrations of glucose (Fig. 3D). The calibration curve based on the R/G ratio versus glucose concentration (0–100µM) can be obtained, the regression equation is Y = 0.0051X + 0.93 with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.9914, where Y and X represent the R/G and the concentration of glucose, respectively. The lowest detectable concentration (LDC) was approximately 3.8µM, which is similar with the detection limit of the above ratiometric fluorescent assays. To our knowledge, this analytical performance can be comparable with or better than some of the reported methods as shown in Table S1, and the advantages of our proposed probe are facile to prepare without fluorescence labeling or chemical modification and the consequence is easy to see by naked eye.

To further test the feasibility of our proposed method in real samples, we studied the possible applicability of the label-free ratiometric probe for the direct measurement of glucose in serum sample. The unknown concentration of glucose in serum sample was measured by our designed probe and the commercialized glucose assay kit. The results were listed in Table S2. Recovery of spiked known amounts of glucose to the serum samples measured by the label-free ratiometric probe was in general larger than 95%, which indicated that the present method has a promise in practical application with great accuracy and reliability.

The selectivity of our proposed method for glucose was evaluated by monitoring F546/F650 of the sensing system induced by other potential interferences including maltose, sucrose, lactose, galactose, fructose and arabinose. As Fig. 4A depicted, it can be seen that only glucose can quench the fluorescence of [G3T]5/Tb3+ while the GelRed/[G3T]5 at 650nm emission was stable. Fig. 4B showed that the fluorescent ratio (F546/F650) decreased significantly and there were negligible fluorescent changes among other analogous. To further confirm the excellent selectivity of our label-free ratiometric probe, competition experiment was made by mixing glucose with other analogous. Fig. 4C showed that the fluorescent spectra in the presence of 100µM glucose mixed with 500µM other analogous was similar to that in the presence of 100µM glucose alone. The fluorescent ratios (F546/F650) from Fig. 4D were consistent with Fig. 4C. These results indicated that the proposed method was highly selective for distinguishing glucose from other sugars. The coexistence of other analogous had no influence on the detection of glucose by our proposed probe.

The successful analysis of glucose by using GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ hybrid and the GOx-biocatalyzed oxidation of glucose suggests the present sensing platform may be implemented for the biocatalytic cascades that involve H2O2-generating oxidase. We have demonstrated the AChE/ChOx bienzyme cascade. Acetylcholine (ACh) is a central neurotransmitter associating with neurological disorders, such as Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, and Huntington's diseases (Miao et al., 2010). AChE-mediated hydrolysis of ACh into choline is the main regulating process of the neural response system (Perry et al., 1999). In AChE/ChOx bienzyme cascade, AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ACh into choline (2), and the resulting choline is then oxidized by ChOx to yield betaine and H2O2 (3), while the enzyme-generated H2O2 is followed by the GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+ reporter system.
(2)
ACh + H
2
O
2
⟶
AChE
Choline
(3)
Choline + O
2
⟶
ChOx
H
2
O
2
+ Betaine

As shown in Fig. S8A, a gradual decrease of fluorescent intensity ratio (F546/F650) occurred with the increasing ACh concentration. Fig. S8B shows a plot of F546/F650 versus ACh concentration. These results confirm that our design is applicable to detect oxidation reaction related biomolecules in biomedical analysis.

In conclusion, a novel label-free ratiometric fluorescent probe has been designed for the detection of H2O2 by hybridization of GelRed and Tb3+ via a designed ssDNA [G3T]5 (i.e. GelRed/[G3T]5/Tb3+). The GelRed/[G3T]5 acts as the internal reference with red emission while [G3T]5/Tb3+ serves as sensitive response unit with green fluorescence, whose fluorescence can be significantly quenched by Hg2+ and recovered by the introduce of Cys. The oxidation of Cys to cystine by H2O2 reverses the Cys-mediated fluorescence changes of [G3T]5/Tb3+, which promises a simple, cost-effective, sensitive and visual detection of H2O2. Additionally, the sensing strategy can be further extended to the monitoring of H2O2-produced oxidase-related reactions, such as GOx-biocatalyzed oxidation of glucose and the (AChE/ChOx) cascade biocatalytic processes, which indicated the broad applications in biological analysis. More significantly, our proposed method has been successfully integrated with a smartphone to make it useful for in-field fast measurement and realtime online analysis.
